suggestedGuidanceUpdates.suggestion: Add note: For memory-bandwidth-limited kernels on gfx906 (MI50/MI60), reducing FMA count via higher-throughput instructions (fdot2, sdot8) does NOT necessarily improve performance. The INT4 GEMV kernel is bandwidth-limited, so v3 (scalar ubfe+FP32-FMA) outperforms v4 (fdot2). Workers should benchmark both approaches when the description says 'try both' rather than assuming higher-throughput instructions always win.
suggestedGuidanceUpdates.evidence: int4-gemv-dot8 worker correctly implemented and tested both approaches. fdot2 v4 is 6-19% slower than v3 scalar because: (1) kernel is HBM bandwidth-limited not compute-limited, (2) nibble→FP16 conversion adds overhead that offsets fdot2 benefit. Worker properly documented this in handoff and architecture.md.

rejectedObservations.observation: INT4 GEMV is memory-bandwidth-limited (100-260 GB/s actual vs 857 GB/s theoretical), making fdot2 slower than scalar
rejectedObservations.reason: already-documented — architecture.md 'INT4 GEMV v4 fdot2 Approach' section documents the root cause analysis in detail

rejectedObservations.observation: v3_t16 is the recommended production INT4 GEMV kernel
rejectedObservations.reason: already-documented — architecture.md 'INT4 GEMV Optimization' section and the v4 section both state this recommendation

rejectedObservations.observation: Polynomial sigmoid is insufficient for SiLU correctness (5e-3 threshold) with typical LLM activations
rejectedObservations.reason: already-documented — architecture.md 'Elementwise Vectorization' section documents exact __expf requirement with explanation

rejectedObservations.observation: float4 bandwidth improvement only applies to RMSNorm, not residual_add or silu_fused at dim=5120
rejectedObservations.reason: already-documented — architecture.md 'Why rmsnorm shows improvement but residual_add does not' explains this with performance data
